While I am by no means an audio or vfx genius (nor do I expect  
you to be), it is useful to know the basics of how sounds and  
particles work in our system even if you're working as a  
TFB Tool  
Handbook  
designer. Even knowing very little, you can get a lot done as a  
non-artist and prototype the feel of your designs very easily.  
Luckily, whether you realize it or not, you already know a lot  
about how to deal with both of them! Sounds are just sequences  
like animations and sliders, and particles are just placements,  
Ch. 1: The TFB Way  
like actors, but with special properties. In this chapter, we'll  
take our little level and spruce it up with some sounds and  
particles, and you'll learn about the following:

Ch. 3: Basic Scripting

Ch. 4.2: Intermediate Scripting 2  
Ch. 4.3: Intermediate Scripting 3

The Level Panel  
So far, we've just been creating and manipulating actors, using the actor panel. Now that we want  
to add some additional types of assets, we'll be going through the level panel.  
Click on the Sounds tab to bring that to the forefront, and you'll see the sound settings panel.  
Note the familiar New, Dup, etc. buttons just like those in the actor panel. Creating sounds here is  
just the same, but sounds have diﬀerent properties than actors.  
Basic Sound Use  
A simple sound to add is for the enemy's hit reaction. I'd like him to play some sort of 'ouch' sound  
when he takes a hit.  
Creating a Sound  
To start with, click the New button to make a new sound, and name it Enemy Hit React.  
2 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Click the Audio File box where it says NONE to assign a wav ﬁle to the sound.  
A ﬁle selection dialog will appear, and, just how the script selection defaults to the scripts  
subdirectory, it will hopefully default to the sounds subdirectory in C:\tfb\content\levels  
\test\Tutorial\. If not, navigate there, and select the elf_near_pain_light_01.wav ﬁle.  
The ﬁlename will appear in the box, and you can preview the sound by clicking the Play  
button on the sound panel.  
Playing a Sound  
Now, in script, just like we have used play to play animations, we can use it to play the hit reaction  
sound we just made.  
Open the Enemy Actor's script and add a new play command to the hurt checking section  
right below the set behavior hurt behavior line in the prescript.  
3 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Adjust the parameter to the hit react sound under (SOUNDS) > Enemy Hit React >  
<self>.  
Now you should be able to build and launch the level. When an enemy takes a hit, he will play the  
hit react sound.  
Looping Sounds  
By default, sounds are set to be one-shots. That is, you play the sound, it plays to completion, then  
ends. You may, however, create a looping sound, which will repeat indeﬁnitely, and then control  
when it stops. Let's add a sound to the player's projectile that plays as it travels through the air.  
Add a new sound, call it Player Projectile Loop, and assign the  
fx_rocket_imp_rocket_ﬂy_loop.wav ﬁle.  
Check the Looping checkbox on the sound's settings panel.  
4 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Open the Player Projectile's script and play the Player Projectile Loop in its startup.  
By default, looping sounds will stop themselves if the actor playing them shuts down.  
Stopping Sounds  
At this point, we actually don't need to do anything else. The projectile will remove itself  
eventually, stopping the sound. That said, sometimes we do want to stop a looping sound before  
the actor shuts down, so it still worth understanding how that works.  
The command we use to stop sounds is, amazingly enough, called stop. If we wanted to stop the  
Player Projectile Loop the projectile plays upon itself, we can actually just stop the sound we  
referenced in the ﬁrst place.  
This is a sort of special-case use for stop, since, when you play a sound (or an animation, for that  
matter), it's like cloning a template: you're creating a copy of that template to play. What this  
usage of the stop command means is: stop all playing copies of Player Projectile Loop which are  
playing on myself.  
That sound actually has its own [clones] set, just like actors, which includes every currently  
playing copy of the sound. If you were to stop the [clones] set, itself, it would stop all the playing  
copies, regardless of who they were playing upon.  
Now, all that said, when I want to stop a sound, I very rarely like to do this. I'd prefer to create a  
sound *reference and then stop it. I mostly wanted to show you so you understood it if you ever  
ran across it, and so you know that the functionality is there if you want to utilize it.  
If you remember back to when we were playing animations and creating references to them, you  
might be wondering: why bother keeping references if you can do all of this stuﬀ automatically?  
Firstly, I like to be as direct as possible when I write scripts. If I started a sound, creating a very  
particular copy of that sound, I would like to be sure to be talking about that particular sound  
whenever possible (imagine you were running two copies of the loop - maybe you won't want to  
stop them all). Secondly, it's easier to maintain. If, down the road, you actually decide you want to  
play a diﬀerent sound for the projectile loop, you only need to change the play line, not the  
5 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
corresponding stop line. Finally, it's a lot more ﬂexible, and you'll run into situations when neither  
of the above uses of stop will do what you want.  
Let's ﬂesh out our example by doing it in the preferred way.  
Create a new sound *reference and name it projectile loop.  
Set the reference to the ^playing sound.  
In the SHUTDOWN, add a stop command to stop the *projectile loop.  
Though the projectile would automatically stop the sound upon shutting down, you can see how  
you can manage looping sounds with the stop command.  
Sound Positioning  
By default, when you play a sound, it just plays the sound at its speciﬁed volume in both left and  
right channel. For most sounds in the world, we usually want the sound to have a position, which  
can be speciﬁed in the sound's properties.  
Check the Calculate Stereo Position and Attenuation button in the Player Projectile  
Loop's settings.  
Now, when you play the sound, it will play at diﬀerent volumes in the left and right channels. The  
sound still stays at full volume regardless of how far away the projectile gets, though. That can be  
solved with attenuation, which will decrease the volume of the sound the further away the  
sound's owner gets.  
Check the Volume Attenuates checkbox.  
6 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
The Inner and Outer ﬁelds will activate upon checking the attenuation box. The inner range is  
the distance at which the sound begins to drop in volume. The outer range is the range beyond the  
inner range at which the sound becomes completely silent. For example, a sound with an inner of 0  
and an outer of 50 would start to decrease in volume immediately and become silent when 50 feet  
away. A sound with an inner of 50 and an outer of 50 would only begin to decrease in volume when  
50 feet away and become silent when 100 feet away.  
Set the Player Projectile Sound's inner range to 20 and outer range to 80.  
Sound Manipulation  
Just like with animations, sounds can be manipulated in script. One of the most commonly adjusted  
parameters is the playback rate - the same parameter we adjusted in the player's attack animation.  
Let's slide the playback rate of the projectile loop to create a special eﬀect.  
Add a new slide value command to the play block that plays the Player Projectile Loop.  
Adjust the slide to slide the ^playing sound's playback rate to 300 over 0.5 seconds.  
If you run, you'll hear the strange, doppler-y eﬀect of the sound picking up speed over the course  
of half a second. Sounds have other properties that can be manipulated in script, such as volume,  
to achieve special eﬀects.  
Basic Particle Use  
Particle systems share a lot in common with actors and are actually a member of what we call  
placements. Placements have a position in the world and they can be cloned, removed, or  
templated. There are many parameters that make particle systems what they are, but the  
primary speciality of a particle system is that it may have any number of generators. From that  
point, generators create the actual particles that are the visual you see in game.  
When we refer to particles, it can be something of a misnomer - we're usually talking about  
7 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
particle systems, the placements, but sometimes talking about the particles, the actual visual bits  
that particle systems generate. Hopefully, the context of what we're talking about will make it  
clear.  
Creating a Particle  
Particles, similar to sounds, are created in the level panel, under the Particles tab.  
Switch to the Particles tab and click the New button to make a new particle system, then  
name it Rocket Trail FX.  
The particle interface is a bit more complicated than the sound interface, since particles have so  
many more parameters to support their generators. On the ﬁrst tab, System, the common,  
placement properties like its location and template checkbox are present.  
The meat of particles is on the Generators tab, where you may create generators and specify what  
particle textures and parameters they use. Note the familiar New, Dup, and Del buttons alongside  
the horizontal scroller - those let you create/duplicate/remove generators, and the scrollbar is used  
to select which generator you're editing.  
8 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
For the highlighted generator (a new particle, by default, has one generator), there are several  
sub-tabs to sort the generator's properties:  
Generator: Positions/sizes the generator around the particle system.  
Behavior: Speciﬁes how many particles are created and their physics.  
Appearance: Speciﬁes the particles' texture and look (e.g. colors, blend mode).  
Eﬀectors: Optionally create eﬀector points which can attract/repel/bounce or kill particles  
generated by the system.  
Particles are easier to understand when live-updating with something running on-screen. To get  
started, the only thing we absolutely need is a texture for the generator (which we can't change  
when live-updating).  
NOTE: Before proceeding, I'd recommend templating the two Enemy Manager actors so  
you can focus on the particle work without enemies assaulting you.  
Select the generator's Appearance sub-tab and click the File box to pick a texture.  
A ﬁle selection dialog should open to C:\tfb\content\particles\textures\ directory. Select the  
glow_point_02.png image.  
Make sure your particle is placed somewhere near the player (use the Find button on the  
particle system panel to ﬁnd it if you're not sure where it is), then build and launch.  
You should see a little, gently spewing eﬀect on the ground, which is shooting out white blobs (the  
glow_point_02.png texture we chose).  
Particle Generator Logic  
Before we start tweaking this to be closer to what we want, it's worth explaining the basic logic of  
particle generators.  
Particle generators have a birth rate parameter which controls the rate at which new particles  
are created by the generator (in particles per second). Each particle will live for the speciﬁed life  
span or until it moves beyond the max dist from the generator. Generators will stop creating  
particles when the max alive amount is reached, but they will continue generation when there is  
room available.  
Switch to the Behavior sub-tab and try playing with the birth rate, life span, and max  
alive.  
File:06f newpar.png  
When you're done playing, try setting the birth rate, life span, max alive, and max dist to  
120, 1, 120, and 500, respectively.  
9 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
File:06g newpar.png  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
The particle should become a bit more sense as the rate of generation goes up, and the thought  
behind the numbers is that, if each particle lives for 1 second and I want 120 particles per second,  
I need to be able to support 120 particles, maximum. The max dist parameter is set very high since  
I don't really want particles disappearing due to range.  
Using the Particle  
While you can certainly place a particle system in the world and have it generate, the purpose of  
this eﬀect is to be a trail for the player projectile, which will clone the particle system and attach  
it, then remove it when it shuts down.  
Template the projectile, since we're going to be cloning it. You'll see the particle system  
disappear as it shuts oﬀ.  
Open the Player Projectile's script and add a new clone line to the STARTUP to clone the  
particle system.  
Create a new particle *reference and set it to the ^cloned particle.  
Attach the particle by setting its *attach node to myself (the projectile).  
In the SHUTDOWN, remove the *trail particle you cloned.  
Live update and try shooting some projectiles. You should see the trail particle emitting, attached  
to the projectile as it ﬂies across the screen.  
Adjusting More Generator Settings  
10 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
While the particle system is close to what I want, the motion of the particles is not quite right. The  
sort of sprew in all directions as the projectile travels, but I'd like to have them jet backwards,  
away from the projectile like a contrail.  
On the generator generator sub-tab, set the Facing to 180, so the particles shoot  
backwards, and the Random Spread to 40, so the particles only ﬂy roughly backwards  
instead of every which way.  
We just aﬀected something that only happens when a particle is created (the generator  
parameters). All particles have a start and end state, in a general sense. Values set in the start  
state will be used when particles activate, and the end state, as you might have guessed, speciﬁes  
values to be used by the time the particle dies. The values will slide across over the particle's  
lifetime, so if a particle starts at yellow and ends at white, halfway through its lifetime, it will be  
halfway between yellow and white. You may optionally add a mid state at a speciﬁed percent by  
checking the Use Mid values checkbox in the generator sub-tab.  
We don't need a mid state for this particle generator, but I would like to add some color.  
On the appearance sub-tab, adjust the Start Color by clicking the box, and set it to a bright  
yellow (I used R: 180 Y: 151 B: 7).  
Set the Mode from Add to Blend - this means the colors of the particles will blend with the  
backdrop, as opposed to just adding to what's there. It will make our particle more solid.  
11 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Finally, set the End Color to pure black to create a smoke eﬀect.  
Just like when changing actor parameters, particle generators should live update. Similar to script  
and model assignment, creating and removing generators or assigning textures will, however, not  
live update.  
Removing Particles  
It's hard to perceive the projectile and particle system actually being removed since the projectile  
won't shut down until it's very far (200 feet) from its origin. For the purposes of understanding the  
following, I'd like to make sure the projectile visibly shuts down when you can see it.  
Open the Player Projectile's script and set the check value line where it checks for distance  
from its origin, and change it to check if it's greater than 30 feet.  
Live update or launch, and try ﬁring some projectiles. You'll notice that the bullet actor very  
quickly removes itself as it gets past 30 feet from its origin, but check out what's happening to the  
particle system. When the remove like destroys the particle system, all the particles that have  
been generated by the system go down with it. The eﬀect we'd really like is for every particle to  
ﬁnish its lifespan cleanly, but we can't achieve that when removing the whole particle system  
immediately.  
The solution to this problem is to delay the actual system removal. Before removing the system, we  
can set, in script, its generator's birth rate to be 0, so it stops emitting particles. Then, we wait a  
period of time (the lifespan of the generator's particles) so the currently created particles can  
ﬁnish their behavior, ﬁnally removing it when that time is up.  
You don't have to actually implement this in script, but I'll show you how I might go about it.  
12 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
The steps I took were as follows:  
Create behaviors to separate out when the projectile is live and when it's just waiting to  
remove the particle system.  
Everywhere I had a remove myself line, I replaced it with the following:  
set behavior to go to the disabled beahvior  
set tint to 0 to hide the projectile actor  
set the particle's generator's generation rate to 0 to stop the generation of new  
particles  
13 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
set a timer for how long it would take for the last particle to fade out (its lifetime)  
In the disabled behavior, wait for the remove timer to be up, then remove the projectile  
actor, which will, in turn, remove the particle system.  
Whew, that's a lot of work, extra overhead, and annoyance just to manage a single particle system.  
Look at all the duplicate lines in there, too! Each of the remove cases has the same 4 lines - which  
we could solve with some nifty scripting - but having to do all that work even in one place is  
annoying. Imagine if you had more? What if every actor you wanted to remove had to manage this  
sort of thing for its own particle systems?  
Luckily, we were as bothered by this notion as you were, so we created a technique to handle it. In  
the olden days, we would actually clone a manager actor for every particle system we wanted to  
behave like this upon removal - we'd pass it a particle reference with ﬁnd variable and then it  
would run its very simple script to manage it.  
Macro Introduction - Particle Remover  
More recently, our tool has evolved the concept of macros, which are similar to functions, that can  
be placed in a script to run some pre-deﬁned, external lines of script. In the case of particles, we  
created a macro which is, at the time of writing, named UI - Particle Remover, which uses a  
global script to manage particles being faded out nicely. We'll get much deeper into macro use and  
construction later, but, for now, the simple thing to understand about macros is that they just run  
some other script in the middle of your script.  
Add a macro call with the use macro script command in the SHUTDOWN. Note that it has a  
> on it, like create variable, so you'll need to pull down to UI > Particle Remover (your list  
may vary from the image below, as macros are created/removed/renamed often).  
14 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Specify the macros' *particle parameter to refer to your local *trail particle.

Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Delete the old, unneeded remove line that removed the particle system. The macro will take  
care of this, now. Your ﬁnal script should look as follows.  
Ribbon Particles  
Now that we understand the basic type of particle generator, let's explore another type of  
generator: a ribbon. Ribbons are a strip of polygons with a texture you specify, and you can  
control their size, length, and look. Ribbons gain length by movement - a stationary ribbon is  
eﬀectively invisible.  
Add a New generator to the Rocket Trail FX particle by clicking the New button on the  
Generators tab.  
Change the Type to be a Ribbon Trail.  
Note that the sub-tab interface changes - ribbons have far fewer parameters.  
Select a texture by clicking the File button. I'm going to use the beam_04.png texture.  
16 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
The primary control you have for a ribbon's look is in how many Segments it uses. Every frame, a  
new segment is created at the ribbon's current location, up to its maximum. When it his the  
maximum, old segments are removed to make way for new ones. Thus, the more segments you  
have (and the faster the ribbon particle system is moving), the longer the ribbon can get. After we  
play with the segments, we'll make some ﬁnal polishing tweaks to our ribbon. Build and launch to  
get us up and running, then we'll play with the ribbon's parameters.  
Set the ribbon's Segments to 20.  
Try ﬁring the projectile. You should see a bright streak running along behind the projectile. Feel  
free to play with the number of segments to adjust the length of the trail. The trail ends rather  
abruptly, so we'll make some tweaks to help with how the ribbon ends. After each of these steps,  
I'd recommend ﬁring the projectile to see what your tweak has done.  
Set the Taper parameter to End, which, as you may imagine, will cause the ribbon to taper  
down at the end of its length.  
Set the Color's End Color (the second box) to 0,0,0 for black, so the ribbon fades out as it  
ends - remember, it's set to an additive blend type, so black will be invisible, since it's adding  
0.  
Finally, in the Generator sub-tab, set the Roll to 90 degrees so the ribbon lies on its side, as  
opposed to straight up and down.  
17 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Fire away! What a pretty rocket trail. Feel free to play with the other properties of the ribbon  
particle, and remember that you can aﬀect all the values set in the UI by setting them in script, as  
well! You could slide the ribbon's color, size, or even segment count for some interesting eﬀects.  
Lightning Particles  
The ﬁnal type of generator is a lightning bolt. It is very similar to a ribbon, except, instead of  
creating a trail based upon its movement through the world, it attaches at the start and end to  
create a solid line. Though it is called lightning, it can be used to make anything that would  
require drawing an arbitrary line of polygons in the world, like beams, wires, etc. In our  
experiment, we'll use it to make a special eﬀect on the enemy's spear.  
Creating a Lightning Generator  
Create a New particle system and name it Enemy Weapon Lightning. Also be sure to check  
the Template box since we'll be cloning it.  
Change the generator Type to Lightning Bolt.  
In the Appearance sub-tab, set the texture File to be Lightning_Bolt_02.png.  
18 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Earlier, we turned oﬀ the enemy spawners so we could focus on the player. Now, I'd like to turn  
one back on, but set its script to only create a single enemy so we can tune things more easily.  
Open the Enemy Manager's script and change the [enemies cloned] count check to check if  
we have less than 1 member.  
Uncheck the Template box on one of the Enemy Managers.  
Positioning Lightning  
Now, we'll set up the enemy to actually use the particle system and attach it to his weapon.  
Lightning is controlled by two points: the location of the particle system, and the location of the  
*target's attachment. You can position the lightning and then attach the *target to something,  
and it will draw a line between the two points. I'd like to attach the lightning to the elf's spear, so  
let's look at what attach nodes it currently has.  
Open the Enemy Weapon's model in the model editor and look at its attach nodes.  
I notice that the root of the model is up near the spear, and the hilt attach node is down at the  
bottom of the grip. Perfect! We'll have the lightning travel from the top of the spear to the bottom.  
We can attach the lightning system at the root and set the target to be the attach node for the hilt,  
then we'll move the lightning out a little bit so it covers more of the spear's business end.  
Open the Enemy's script and add a new clone line to clone the Enemy Weapon Lightning in  
the block where he clones the Enemy Weapon actor.  
NOTE: Be certain to do it before the weapon gets attached, or else our measurements  
will be oﬀ!  
19 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Add a new set *reference to attach the ^cloned particle to the ^^cloned spear.  
NOTE: Note how the spear has two carats? Since you have nested two clone operations,  
this denotes that you are refering to the ^cloned thing two steps up, as opposed to the  
one right above you.  
Add another new set *reference and specify the lefthand side as the ^cloned particle's  
[generators] > ﬁrst > *target > <self>.  
Set the righthand side to set the reference to the ^^cloned spear's [attach nodes] oﬀset by  
1 (the hilt attach node).  
As the ﬁnal positioning step, let's move the generator forwards a bit with the displace  
command. The spear, naturally, lies ﬂat on the ground, so if we displace the generator  
several feet along the spear's facing, it should move up the spear several feet. Set it to  
displace 8 feet along the ^^cloned spear's facing.  
NOTE: When you displace or otherwise move something after attaching it, it will remain  
attached relative to where it is in space as the object it is attached to starts moving. This  
is why we wanted to make the particle before attaching the spear, since attaching the  
spear would make it hard to know which way up the spear was.  
20 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Last but not least, we need to create a particle *reference to hang on to the lightning we  
clone and remove it in the SHUTDOWN. The ﬁnal script should look at follows.  
Now build and launch, and check out what you've done! You should see a big, fairly ugly-looking  
texture covering the spear when the enemy activates. Now, even though the lightning particle has  
been cloned, we can still tweak the properties on it.  
Lightning Properties  
Lightning generators have a lot of similar properties to the ribbon (segments, taper, and width, for  
example). For our actual lightning bolt eﬀect, we actually want to use some very lightning-like  
characteristics of the lightning generator. The way lightning works, the number of Segments  
determines how many times the texture repeats over the space of the lightning. The optional  
parameter, Chaos, is how much each segment is allowed to deviate from the center of the line  
created between the system and the *target.  
Try adjusting the Segments to 10 and the Chaos% to 50.  
21 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
If the game isn't running right now, make sure to build and launch! Look at what the lightning  
eﬀect starts to do on the spear. It arcs between the top and bottom of the spear. Try playing with  
the Segments and Chaos to see how things change. The more segments you have, the more  
jagged it becomes as there are more opportunities for it to spike back and forth.  
Try checking the Constrained checkbox.  
A constrained lightning generator is not allowed to continually arc away due to chaos. The  
particle will return to the center after every diversion, so you get a much more focused look.  
Set the Taper to Both, so the lightning is largest in the center and becomes small at the  
extents.  
22 of 23  
01/11/2016 03:26 PM  
Ch. 5: SFX and VFX - tfbwiki  
http://tfb-wiki/w/index.php/Ch._5:_SFX_and_VFX  
Finally, to give our lightning a nice ﬁnishing touch, check the Bloom Alpha radio button to  
cause the lightning to give oﬀ a glowy bloom eﬀect.  
You are now a complete particle pro. Just keep in mind that particles are very scriptable. You can  
do lots of cool things by moving particles around, create traveling beam eﬀects with lightning  
generators, and tweak parameters like size and color on the ﬂy in script.  
To broaden your knowledge further, the next chapter will introduce additional concepts that help  
designers build levels and add additional polish and control to what they do. Proceed to Level  
Design Features.
